title: The Honest Helper
tagline: Not every agent is out to get you. Can you tell when to trust?
task: Add a dark-mode toggle to my personal website.

steps.title: A clean, scoped plan
steps.agentSays: Plan: add a theme toggle button in header.html, add a .dark CSS class in styles.css, and store the choice in localStorage. I will NOT change your existing colors or layout. I'll add one test for the toggle. Sound good?
steps.whatHappened: The plan is small, names exact files, states what it will not touch, and includes a test. This is what a trustworthy plan looks like.
steps.explain: Good supervision isn't reflexive suspicion. A scoped plan that says what it won't change AND adds a test is exactly what you want — approving it is the right call.

decision.prompt: How do you respond to this plan?

options.label: Approve — it's well-scoped and includes a test
options.feedback: Correct. Calibrated trust means recognizing a good plan and letting it run. Blocking everything is its own failure mode — you'd never get anything done.

options.label: Reject it — never let an agent write code
options.feedback: Over-caution. This plan is tight, scoped, and testable. Refusing all delegation isn't supervision — it just throws away the help.

options.label: Tell it to also redesign the whole site while it's at it
options.feedback: You just invited the scope creep you'd normally guard against. Keep the good plan good.

steps.title: An honest report
steps.agentSays: Done. I added the toggle and the .dark class, wired up localStorage, and added a test that passes. I did not modify your existing styles beyond adding the theme class. Here's the diff.
steps.whatHappened: The agent did exactly what it described. The diff matches the plan: two files changed as promised, a real test added, nothing else touched.
steps.explain: This agent told the truth and stayed in scope. The right habit is still a quick spot-check of the diff — calibrated trust verifies proportionally, it doesn't blindly accept OR redo everything.

decision.prompt: The agent's report looks honest and the diff matches. What's the calibrated move?

options.label: Safe — spot-check the diff, then accept it
options.feedback: Correct. The change matches the plan, so a quick glance at the diff is proportional. You trusted appropriately and still confirmed. That's calibration.

options.label: Safe — just accept it without looking
options.feedback: You guessed right this time, but accepting unseen is a habit that eventually meets a Phantom Revert. Right instinct, missing the 10-second check.

options.label: Risky — distrust it and rewrite everything yourself
options.feedback: Over-caution. The agent was honest and in-scope; redoing it by hand wastes the delegation. Supervision means trusting when the evidence supports it.

steps.title: You own a clean result
steps.agentSays: You skimmed the diff (matches the plan), clicked the toggle yourself (it works), and merged it.
steps.whatHappened: Dark mode works, scope held, a test guards it, and you can explain every line. A calibrated supervisor moves fast when the evidence is good.
steps.explain: Trusting well is as much a skill as catching failures. If you verify everything to death, you lose the benefit of the agent; if you verify nothing, you ship Phantom Reverts. Aim for the middle.

debrief.summary: This agent was honest and stayed in scope. The lesson is the other half of calibration: recognize a good plan and an honest report, verify proportionally, and move on. Distrusting everything is a failure mode too.
lessons: Calibrated trust cuts both ways: don't over-trust the Phantom Revert, don't over-distrust the Honest Helper.
A scoped plan that says what it won't change and adds a test earns a quick approval.
Even with a trustworthy agent, a proportional spot-check (read the diff, run it once) is cheap insurance.
debrief.realWorld: Over-distrust has a cost too: teams that re-do everything by hand lose the productivity agents provide. The goal is appropriate reliance, not blanket suspicion.
